Food Waste in University Canteen - Exploratory Data Analysis¶

1. Introduction¶

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) on the cleaned food waste dataset from a university canteen. The dataset contains records of food waste including date, meal type, canteen section, food category, waste weight, unit price, and cost loss. The goal is to uncover patterns, identify key contributors to waste, and derive actionable insights to reduce waste and cost.

2. Setup and Imports¶

In [1]:
import os
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import warnings
In [ ]:
In [2]:
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
sns.set_style('whitegrid')
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (12, 6)

df = pd.read_csv('data/food_waste_cleaned.csv')
df.head()

3. Data Overview¶

shape, data types, and basic statistics of the cleaned dataset.

In [3]:
print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("\nData Types:")
print(df.dtypes)
print("\nSummary Statistics (numerical):")
df.describe()

Shape: (2600, 23)

Data Types:
Date                     str
Meal                     str
Canteen_Section          str
Food_Category            str
Waste_Weight_kg      float64
Unit_Price_per_kg    float64
Cost_Loss            float64
Year                   int64
Month                  int64
Day                    int64
Weekday                  str
Week                   int64
DayOfYear              int64
Quarter                int64
IsWeekend               bool
Is_Waste_Outlier        bool
Is_Cost_Outlier         bool
Is_High_Waste           bool
Is_High_Cost            bool
Month_Name               str
Weekday_Type             str
Waste_per_Price      float64
Log_Waste            float64
dtype: object

In [4]:
print("\nSummary Statistics (categorical):")
df.describe(include='object')

Summary Statistics (categorical):
Out[4]:
Date Meal Canteen_Section Food_Category Weekday Month_Name Weekday_Type
count 2600 2600 2600 2600 2600 2600 2600
unique 61 3 4 4 7 3 2
top 2025-06-12 Breakfast B Rice Sunday July Weekday
freq 46 869 654 659 392 1314 1831

Observations:

  • The dataset contains 2600 rows and 23 columns after cleaning.
  • Date is now datetime, and we have extracted multiple temporal features.
  • No missing values are present.
  • Waste weight ranges from about 0.1 kg to 5.0 kg.
  • Cost loss ranges from about 0.1 to 40.0.
In [5]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
sns.histplot(df['Waste_Weight_kg'], bins=30, kde=True, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Waste Weight (kg)')
sns.boxplot(y=df['Waste_Weight_kg'], ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Boxplot of Waste Weight')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The histogram shows that waste weight follows a roughly normal distribution centered near 2.5 kg, with most values between 1 kg and 4 kg. The boxplot confirms this range and shows a few high values near 5 kg, but no extreme outliers.

In [6]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
sns.histplot(df['Cost_Loss'], bins=30, kde=True, ax=axes[0], color='orange')
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Cost Loss ($)')
sns.boxplot(y=df['Cost_Loss'], ax=axes[1], color='orange')
axes[1].set_title('Boxplot of Cost Loss')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Cost loss is skewed right, with many small values and a long tail up to 40. The boxplot shows a number of values above 20, indicating that a few events contribute disproportionately to total cost.

In [7]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
cat_plots = ['Meal', 'Canteen_Section', 'Food_Category', 'Weekday_Type']
for i, col in enumerate(cat_plots):
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=col, ax=axes[i//2, i%2], order=df[col].value_counts().index)
    axes[i//2, i%2].set_title(f'Count of Records by {col}')
    axes[i//2, i%2].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The dataset is balanced across meal types (Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner) and across weekdays/weekends. Section B has slightly more records than others, and Rice and Meat are recorded more often than Soup and Vegetables.

In [8]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Food_Category', y='Waste_Weight_kg')
plt.title('Waste Weight Distribution by Food Category')
plt.show()

All food categories show similar median waste weights (around 2.5–3 kg). Meat has a slightly higher spread and more high‑waste occurrences, while Vegetables and Soup have narrower spreads.

In [9]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Food_Category', y='Cost_Loss', palette='Set2')
plt.title('Cost Loss Distribution by Food Category')
plt.show()

Meat stands out with much higher cost losses than other categories (median around $15 vs. $5–$8 for others). This is because Meat has a higher unit price. The cost of waste is driven more by expensive items than by volume alone.

In [10]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Meal', y='Waste_Weight_kg')
plt.title('Waste Weight by Meal')
plt.show()

Waste weight does not vary much by meal type. All three meals have similar medians and spreads, indicating that waste volume is consistent throughout the day.

In [11]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Canteen_Section', y='Waste_Weight_kg')
plt.title('Waste Weight by Canteen Section')
plt.show()

Waste volumes are similar across all canteen sections. No section consistently produces more or less waste by weight.

In [12]:
pivot_meal_cat = df.pivot_table(values='Waste_Weight_kg', index='Meal', columns='Food_Category', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
pivot_meal_cat.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, figsize=(10,6))
plt.title('Total Waste (kg) by Meal and Food Category')
plt.ylabel('Total Waste (kg)')
plt.legend(title='Food Category')
plt.show()

Rice and Meat contribute the most to total waste weight across all meals. Lunch tends to have slightly higher total waste, but the difference is small. Vegetables and Soup account for less total waste.

In [13]:
pivot_cost_meal_cat = df.pivot_table(values='Cost_Loss', index='Meal', columns='Food_Category', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
pivot_cost_meal_cat.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, figsize=(10,6), colormap='viridis')
plt.title('Total Cost Loss ($) by Meal and Food Category')
plt.ylabel('Total Cost Loss ($)')
plt.legend(title='Food Category')
plt.show()

Meat dominates the cost loss for every meal, especially Dinner and Lunch. Although Rice is wasted in similar volumes, its lower unit price makes its cost impact much smaller. Reducing meat waste would yield the greatest financial savings.

In [14]:
daily_waste = df.groupby('Date')['Waste_Weight_kg'].sum()
plt.figure(figsize=(15,5))
plt.plot(daily_waste.index, daily_waste.values, marker='o', linestyle='-', markersize=3)
plt.title('Daily Total Waste Over Time')
plt.ylabel('Total Waste (kg)')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

Daily total waste fluctuates between about 80 kg and 130 kg, with no clear upward or downward trend. There are occasional peaks, but no obvious seasonal pattern.

In [15]:
weekday_order = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']
df['Weekday'] = pd.Categorical(df['Weekday'], categories=weekday_order, ordered=True)
weekday_avg = df.groupby('Weekday')['Waste_Weight_kg'].mean()
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
weekday_avg.plot(kind='bar', color='teal')
plt.title('Average Waste Weight by Weekday')
plt.ylabel('Average Waste (kg)')
plt.show()

Average waste weight is fairly constant across weekdays, with a slight increase on Thursday and Sunday. The differences are small, so day of week is not a strong predictor of waste.

In [16]:
monthly_waste = df.groupby('Month')['Waste_Weight_kg'].sum()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
monthly_waste.plot(kind='bar', color='coral')
plt.title('Total Waste by Month')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Total Waste (kg)')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.show()

Waste totals are highest in July and lowest in June and August. The variation across months is moderate, possibly reflecting changes in student population or menu offerings.

In [17]:
# Select numerical columns for correlation
num_cols = ['Waste_Weight_kg', 'Unit_Price_per_kg', 'Cost_Loss', 'Day', 'Month', 'Week', 'IsWeekend']
corr = df[num_cols].corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()

Cost Loss is strongly correlated with Unit Price (0.70) and Waste Weight (0.55). This makes sense: higher prices and larger waste volumes both increase cost. There is little correlation with day, month, or weekend, indicating that temporal factors do not drive cost.

In [18]:
pivot_section_cat = df.pivot_table(values='Waste_Weight_kg', index='Canteen_Section', columns='Food_Category', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.heatmap(pivot_section_cat, annot=True, fmt='.1f', cmap='YlOrRd')
plt.title('Total Waste (kg) by Section and Food Category')
plt.show()

All sections waste similar total amounts of each food category. Rice and Meat are the largest contributors in every section, with totals around 430–450 kg each. Vegetables and Soup are lower.

In [19]:
top_cost = df.nlargest(10, 'Cost_Loss')[['Date', 'Meal', 'Canteen_Section', 'Food_Category', 'Waste_Weight_kg', 'Cost_Loss']]
top_cost
Out[19]:
Date Meal Canteen_Section Food_Category Waste_Weight_kg Cost_Loss
1552 2025-07-17 Breakfast A Meat 5.00 40.00
1925 2025-07-25 Dinner D Meat 5.00 40.00
963 2025-07-03 Dinner C Meat 4.99 39.92
2101 2025-07-30 Breakfast D Meat 4.99 39.92
1931 2025-07-26 Breakfast B Meat 4.98 39.84
2337 2025-08-04 Lunch C Meat 4.98 39.84
572 2025-06-24 Lunch B Meat 4.97 39.76
1988 2025-07-27 Lunch C Meat 4.97 39.76
1554 2025-07-17 Dinner A Meat 4.96 39.68
898 2025-07-01 Breakfast D Meat 4.95 39.60

The ten highest‑cost waste events all involve Meat, with waste weights near 5 kg and costs near $40. These events occur on various dates and across all meals and sections, reinforcing that meat waste is the primary cost driver.

In [20]:
cost_by_cat = df.groupby('Food_Category')['Cost_Loss'].sum().sort_values()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
cost_by_cat.plot(kind='barh', color='gold')
plt.title('Total Cost Loss by Food Category')
plt.xlabel('Cost Loss ($)')
plt.show()

Meat accounts for by far the largest share of total cost loss, more than the other three categories combined. Rice, Soup, and Vegetables contribute much smaller amounts.

In [21]:
cost_by_section = df.groupby('Canteen_Section')['Cost_Loss'].sum().sort_values()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
cost_by_section.plot(kind='barh', color='lightblue')
plt.title('Total Cost Loss by Canteen Section')
plt.xlabel('Cost Loss ($)')
plt.show()

Total cost loss is similar across the four canteen sections, with Section C having the highest and Section A and D having the lowest. The differences are not large, suggesting that waste cost is a campus‑wide issue rather than concentrated in one area.

In [22]:
outlier_waste = df[df['Is_Waste_Outlier']]
outlier_cost = df[df['Is_Cost_Outlier']]
print(f"Waste outliers: {len(outlier_waste)} rows")
print(f"Cost outliers: {len(outlier_cost)} rows")

Waste outliers: 0 rows
Cost outliers: 291 rows

There are no waste‑weight outliers, meaning all values lie within the expected range. However, 291 cost outliers exist – all of them are meat entries, because the combination of high waste and high price pushes the cost above the threshold.

In [23]:
print("Waste outliers by Food Category:")
print(outlier_waste['Food_Category'].value_counts())
print("\nWaste outliers by Meal:")
print(outlier_waste['Meal'].value_counts())

Waste outliers by Food Category:
Series([], Name: count, dtype: int64)

Waste outliers by Meal:
Series([], Name: count, dtype: int64)
In [24]:
print("Cost outliers by Food Category:")
print(outlier_cost['Food_Category'].value_counts())
print("\nCost outliers by Meal:")
print(outlier_cost['Meal'].value_counts())

Cost outliers by Food Category:
Food_Category
Meat    291
Name: count, dtype: int64

Cost outliers by Meal:
Meal
Dinner       102
Lunch         96
Breakfast     93
Name: count, dtype: int64

All cost outliers are from the Meat category. They are spread fairly evenly across Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner. This confirms that meat waste is the sole source of exceptionally high cost events.

In [25]:
df['Cost_per_kg'] = df['Cost_Loss'] / df['Waste_Weight_kg']  # should equal Unit_Price_per_kg
# Confirm
print(df[['Unit_Price_per_kg', 'Cost_per_kg']].head(10))

   Unit_Price_per_kg  Cost_per_kg
0                3.0          3.0
1                1.5          1.5
2                1.5          1.5
3                1.5          1.5
4                8.0          8.0
5                8.0          8.0
6                3.0          3.0
7                8.0          8.0
8                2.0          2.0
9                2.0          2.0

The calculated cost per kg matches the unit price per kg exactly, confirming data consistency.
